tipRules.tip: Robertson: Use a Ned rig with a 1/4-ounce mushroom-style jighead and green-pumpkin Ned Ocho on spinning gear (10-pound braid to 6-pound fluorocarbon leader) to target smallmouth holding on boulders in 20 feet of water where baitfish are present.

Robertson: Pair a Neko rig (size 2 Gamakatsu circle hook, 6.5-inch finesse worm in green-pumpkin) with a Ned rig when targeting smallmouth on deep boulders. Work both presentations in a 60-foot area where fish are concentrated.

Robertson: When a deep pattern dies due to wind shift muddying the water, switch to a modified walking topwater (Sexy Dawg with SuspenDots and upgraded treble hooks to reduce buoyancy) targeting bass in 1-4 feet of water in drains at the backs of pockets, casting to any distinctive rock or wood cover.

Robertson: Adding Storm SuspenDots and replacing stock hooks with heavier Japanese trebles (Yogi) on a walking topwater like the Sexy Dawg pulls the belly down for a different action. Fish are more apt to get the bait and hooks instead of blowing it out of the water.

knowledge.insight: Robertson: A wind direction change can destroy a deep-water pattern by muddying previously clear water. When visibility drops from 6 feet to 2 feet, bass stop moving through the area and the deep bite dies. Be ready to switch to a shallow backup plan.
Robertson: In fall, smallmouth stack up on boulder fields in 20 feet of water where baitfish are concentrated. The fish can be found within a very small 60-foot area when they're grouped up.

Robertson: Adding SuspenDots and heavier aftermarket treble hooks to a walking topwater bait reduces buoyancy so the belly rides lower in the water. This gives the bait a different action and results in better hookup rates because fish get the bait and hooks instead of blowing it out of the water.

Robertson: The prespawn period is one of Robertson's strongest seasonal patterns, complementing his offshore fishing identity. He considers himself a 'heavyweight angler' who prefers big-fish fisheries.
